title: Opinion: Liège-Bastogne-Liège showed why Paul Seixas cannot move to UAE Team Emirates-XRG
content:
Cyclist
Opinion: Liège-Bastogne-Liège showed why Paul Seixas cannot move to UAE Team Emirates-XRG

A rider sweeping all the Monuments in one season. Ample coverage for the women’s peloton. Someone with the ability to follow Tadej Pogačar. All rare sights in cycling, yet the latter was on full display during the men’s Liège-Bastogne-Liège at the weekend.

Paul Seixas is in the midst of a meteoric rise. Despite only being 19 years old, he has firmly claimed the mantle as France’s next big hope, and fanfare for a potential debut in the Tour de France is already building.

He is under contract with French team Decathlon CMA CGM through 2027, but his stock has been rising faster than his ascent up the Mur de Huy to win La Flèche Wallonne, and lurking in the wings with the biggest chequebook in cycling are UAE Team Emirates-XRG. However, a transfer to UAE would be the wrong move for the young Frenchman.

There’s an expectation that Pogačar will win every race he enters. It’s not an entirely foregone conclusion – just glance at the results sheet from Paris-Roubaix the past two seasons – but all too often there simply stands no credible challenger. He mops up Monuments, Grand Tours and World Championships while making it look easy. And it was assumed the same thing would happen at Liège-Bastogne-Liège on Sunday.

Pogačar had claimed two of his three previous Liège titles through solo attacks on the Côte de la Redoute around the 30km-to-go mark, and this year the race looked to be heading that way once again.

Except this time Seixas entered the equation.

For so long, Pogačar has been all but unchallenged in the hilly Classics, romping away to countless victories and dispatching some of the world’s best riders with ease. However, Seixas had already showed he could put up a fight at Strade Bianche, where he was indeed distanced on the Monte Sante Marie gravel sector but still finished second behind Pogačar and in front of the Slovenian’s teammate Isaac del Toro. At Liège, Pogačar readied himself for another solo escapade, but the Côte de la Redoute came and went, and Seixas remained.

Crowds witnessed the duo powering by over the remaining climbs at record speed, engaged in the kind of battle that is becoming increasingly rare: Pogačar and someone. It could’ve been anyone, but it’s significant that it’s the youthful Seixas – still the second youngest rider in the WorldTour – who has stepped up to take the mantle where others could not.

And that is exactly why it would be a such a shame for Seixas to end up on UAE Team Emirates-XRG. While they are one of only a few teams with the resources for a reported €8m pricetag, they are also known for sweeping up top talent and turning them into domestiques. The likes of Florian Vermeersch, Jhonatan Narváez and Benoît Cosnefroy could all be top dogs elsewhere but have chosen to race in aid of Pogačar. Del Toro has mostly escaped this narrative, partly because he started his pro career with the team, and partly because he almost won the Giro last year, which secured his place as the team’s chief alternative GC option when Pogačar isn’t on the start line.

But when it comes to Seixas, having him become just another cook in Pogačar’s kitchen will deprive cycling of what it truly needs. At its core, sport entertains. It rakes emotions over the coals. There’s plenty to romanticise about a French rider on a French team winning the Tour de France. There’s even more to get excited about at the prospect of the Tour, the Monuments, and any other race Pogačar is in becoming competitive again. And that just isn’t going to happen if Seixas joins UAE while Pogačar is at his peak and he is still so early in his career.

Put simply, Pogačar vs Seixas could develop into the battle the sport needs, and it might never happen if they’re both riding for UAE.

The post Opinion: Liège-Bastogne-Liège showed why Paul Seixas cannot move to UAE Team Emirates-XRG appeared first on Cyclist.
author: Robyn Davidson | Editorial Assistant
